skillName: Understand angle notation and label angles correctly
misconceptionSummary: Students read ∠ABC and incorrectly think A is the vertex instead of the middle letter B.

lines.text: Student: M is the vertex (reads left to right)  ✗
The first letter is NOT the vertex

steps.narration: The student read angle MNP left to right and assumed M is the vertex. This is a natural but incorrect assumption. In angle notation, reading order does not place the vertex first — the vertex is always the middle letter.

visuals.ruleText: Vertex = middle letter — always, regardless of reading order
visuals.subText: ∠MNP → N is the vertex.  ∠ABC → B is the vertex.

steps.narration: In three-letter angle notation, the vertex is always the middle letter. It does not matter which order the letters are written — the one in the middle names the corner of the angle.

lines.text: ∠MNP → middle letter N → vertex is N  ✓
Arms run from N toward M and from N toward P
∠PNM names the same angle — N still in middle

steps.narration: Angle MNP: the middle letter is N, so the vertex is N. The arms run from N toward M and from N toward P. Note that angle PNM names the same angle — reversing the outer letters is allowed, because N remains in the middle.

visuals.label: Middle letter = vertex — the order of outer letters does not matter

steps.narration: The vertex is N in both cases, because N is the middle letter. Whether you write MNP or PNM, the angle is the same. Always look at the middle letter to find the vertex.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: taking the first letter as the vertex in ∠MNP. The vertex is always the MIDDLE letter — N in ∠MNP. Reading left to right is a trap; the convention puts the vertex in the middle.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Do not read angle notation like a word — the vertex is not the first letter. It is always the middle letter. In ∠MNP, the vertex is N. In ∠ABC, the vertex is B.
